questions.text: According to the liquidity preference theory, an increase in the overall price level of 10 percent

options.text: increases the equilibrium interest rate, which in turn decreases the quantity of goods and services demanded.
decreases the equilibrium interest rate, which in turn increases the quantity of goods and services demanded.
increases the quantity of money supplied by 10 percent, leaving the interest rate and the quantity of goods and services demanded unchanged.
decreases the quantity of money demanded by 10 percent, leaving the interest rate and the quantity of goods and services demanded unchanged.

questions.text: In which of the following cases would the quantity of money demanded be largest?
According to the theory of liquidity preference, which variable adjusts to balance the supply and demand for money?

options.text: interest rate
money supply
quantity of output

price level Figure 34-2. On the left-hand graph, MS represents the supply of money and MD represents the demand for money; on the right-hand graph, AD represents aggregate demand. The usual quantities are measured along the axes of both graphs.

questions.text: Refer to Figure 34-2. A decrease in Y from Y1 to Y2 is explained as follows:

options.text: The Federal Reserve increases the money supply, causing the money-demand curve to shift from MD1 to MD2; this shift of MD causes r to increase from r1 to r2; and this increase in r causes Y to decrease from Y1 to Y2.
An increase in P from P1 to P2 causes the money-demand curve to shift from MD1 to MD2; this shift of MD causes r to increase from r1 to r2; and this increase in r causes Y to decrease from Y1 to Y2.
A decrease in P from P2 to P1 causes the money-demand curve to shift from MD1 to MD2; this shift of MD causes r to increase from r1 to r2; and this increase in r causes Y to decrease from Y1 to Y2.
An increase in the price level causes the money-demand curve to shift from MD2 to MD1; this shift of MD causes r to decrease from r2 to r1; and this decrease in r causes Y to decrease from Y1 to Y2.

questions.text: Refer to Figure 34-2. As we move from one point to another along the money-demand curve MD1,

options.text: the price level is held fixed at P1.
the interest rate is held fixed at r1.
the money supply is changing so as to keep the money market in equilibrium.
the expected inflation rate is changing so as to keep the real interest rate constant.

questions.text: Refer to Figure 34-2. Assume the money market is always in equilibrium, and suppose r1 = 0.08; r2 = 0.12; Y1 = 13,000; Y2 = 10,000; P1 = 1.0; and P2 = 1.2. Which of the following statements is correct? When P = P2,

options.text: investment is lower than it is when P = P1.
nominal output is higher than it is when P = P1.
the expected rate of inflation is higher than it is when P = P1.
the velocity of money is higher than it is when P = P1.

questions.text: According to liquidity preference theory, if the quantity of money demanded is greater than the quantity supplied, then the interest rate will

options.text: increase and the quantity of money demanded will decrease.
increase and the quantity of money demanded will increase.
decrease and the quantity of money demanded will decrease.
decrease and the quantity of money demanded will increase.

questions.text: Which of the following events would shift money demand to the right?

options.text: an increase in the interest rate or an increase in the price level
an increase in the interest rate, but not an increase in the price level
an increase in the price level, but not an increase in the interest rate
neither an increase in the interest rate nor an increase in the price level

questions.text: Which of the following events would shift money demand to the left?

options.text: an increase in the interest rate or an increase in the price level
an increase in the interest rate, but not an increase in the price level
an increase in the price level, but not an increase in the interest rate
neither an increase in the interest rate nor an increase in the price level

questions.text: The multiplier for changes in government spending is calculated as

options.text: MPC.
1 - MPC.
1/MPC.
1/(1 - MPC). Figure 34-4. On the figure, MS represents money supply and MD represents money demand.

questions.text: Refer to Figure 34-4. A shift of the money-demand curve from MD2 to MD1 is consistent with which of the following sets of events?

options.text: The government cuts taxes, resulting in an increase in people’s incomes.
The government reduces government spending, resulting in a decrease in people’s incomes.
The Federal Reserve increases the supply of money, which decreases the interest rate.
All of the above are correct.

questions.text: An increase in government spending initially and primarily shifts

options.text: aggregate demand to the right.
aggregate demand to the left.
aggregate supply to the right.
neither aggregate demand nor aggregate supply in either direction.
increase, then consumption increases, and aggregate demand shifts rightward.
increase, then consumption decreases, and aggregate demand shifts leftward.
decrease, then consumption increases, and aggregate demand shifts leftward.
decrease, then consumption decreases, and aggregate demand shifts rightward.

questions.text: If businesses and consumers become pessimistic, the Federal Reserve can attempt to reduce the impact on the price level and real GDP by

options.text: increasing the money supply, which raises interest rates.
increasing the money supply, which lowers interest rates.
decreasing the money supply, which raises interest rates.
decreasing the money supply, which lowers interest rates.

questions.text: Which of the following policies would be advocated by proponents of stabilization policy when the economy is experiencing severe unemployment?

options.text: a decrease in the money supply
a reduction in tax rates
a decrease in government purchases
None of the above is correct. Figure 34-6.

questions.text: Refer to Figure 34-6. Which of the following is correct?

options.text: Unemployment rises as the economy moves from point a to point b.
Either fiscal or monetary policy could be used to move the economy from point b to point a.
If the economy is left alone, then as the economy moves from point b to long-run equilibrium, the price level will fall farther.
All of the above are correct.
